items.title: Why Trump's racist video of the Obamas might have minimal consequences for the midterms | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US President Donald Trump has condemned -- but not apologized -- for posting a racist video depicting the Obamas as apes on his social media platform. The White House initially defended the video. But after a massive backlash, it was deleted hours later in a rare backtrack.

items.title: Over 100,000 Ukrainians left without power in freezing conditions following latest attack
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Hundreds of thousands of Ukrainians have been left without electricity or heating in sub-zero temperatures, after Russia launched its latest attack on Ukraine's power grid. Kyiv says the overnight drone and missile strikes targeted electricity generation and distribution facilities across the country. The attacks come just days after what was touted as a promising round of peace talks between the warring parties. 

Chapters:
0:00 Russian attacks on Ukraine power grid
2:09 Marina Miron, Security analyst

items.title: Thai elections: Hope for change, or an unstable future awaits? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Thailand is heading into a closely watched election that could test the country’s long-promised return to stable civilian rule. The polls are seen as a three-way race between conservatives,  populists and the progressive People’s Party, which polls suggest is picking up support from younger voters. Thailand’s parliament is deeply fragmented, and a coalition government is the most likely outcome. DW's southeast Asia correspondent Georg Matthes is in the Thai capital Bangkok . 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Heavy rains and melting snow have washed tons of garbage into Bosnia’s Drina River, including plastic, medical waste, and dead animals. Activists warn the pollution threatens health and blame illegal dumps across Bosnia, Serbia, and Montenegro.

items.title: Voters in Japan prepare for snap election: What's at stake | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>In Japan, voters will head to the polls on Sunday - in a snap election called by Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi.The conservative leader called the vote in the hope of strengthening her party’s position in parliament. Opinion polls suggest that gamble may pay off, with many voters drawn to her direct style and focus on cost-of-living pressures.

00:00 Japanese head to polls in snap election

items.title: How Indian students end up exploited in Germany | Business Beyond
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Many Indian students who came to Germany with high hopes of climbing the career ladder are facing a harsh reality check when they get here. Shackled by high university fees and expensive housing, they often end up working as food delivery riders to make ends meet. But the industry is often underpinned by shady practices, involving anonymous subcontractors, subpar working conditions and pay below the legal minimum wage. So far, German authorities appear to be struggling to address the problem. On this episode of Business Beyond, we join the dots to find out how this exploitative system has been allowed to endure and what could be done to improve conditions for platform workers.

Chapters:
00:00 Why come to Germany? 
01:00 Food Delivery Business 
02:29 The Origin Story
07:20 How is it allowed to happen?  
13:00 Solutions 
14:53 Conclusion 


#FoodDelivery #Lieferando #BusinessBeyond

items.title: Bangladesh to hold first elections since Sheikh Hasina’s overthrow | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>On February 12, Bangladeshis will go to the polls for the first time since Sheikh Hasina's government was ousted a year and a half ago. Hasina was ousted by a student-led movement which has formed a party called the Jatiya Nagarik Party (JNP), also known as the National Citizen Party (NCP). NCP leader, Nahid Islam, speaks about the challenges the party faces in forming political alliances and gaining support. Islam says the upcoming election is incredibly consequential: "this is no ordinary vote. It is not just about changing the government, but changing the country itself." NCP has formed a political alliance with the patriarchal Jamaat-e-Islami party, which has faced some backlash, especially due to its reputation concerning women's rights. 
The NCP's support is concentrated around the capital Dhaka, and faces challenges in broadening its support base. A majority of 128 million Bangladesh voters are Gen-Z.

Chapter Breakdown
0:00 DW's Zobaer Ahmed reports from Dhaka
4:46 Arafat Ahmed, Bangladeshi activist, on challenges facing the student-led NCP 
8:51 Naomi Hossain, Professor of Development Studies at SOAS, University of London, in low-polling numbers

items.title: Why Iran is unwilling to discuss its ballistic missiles program in nuclear talks with US | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>On Friday, high-stakes US-Iran talks were mediated by Oman's Foreign Minister Badr bin Hamad Al Busaidi. There has been disagreement on the scope of the talks, with Iran wanting to focus exclusively on nuclear enrichment, and the US pushing for talks on Iran's ballistic missiles program. Nevertheless, the talks got off to a positive start, according to Iran's Foreign Minister Araghchi.

items.title: EU gives TikTok an ultimatum: Change 'addictive' design or face fines | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>On Friday, the European Commission took aim at the most popular social media platform among young people, the Chinese-owned TikTok, saying its design is addictive. The European Commission says TikTok violates Europe's online safety rules and suggested the app disable what is known as infinite scroll. Infinite Scroll allows people to quickly cycle through millions of videos. TikTok rejects the claims, calling them categorically false. But the tide of public opinion is changing. Countries are banning social media for young people altogether.  If TikTok does not change its design, it could be hit by massive fines.

DW Correspondent Teri Schultz in Brussels reports on the European Commission's accusations against TikTok. 

        
#euonlinesafety
#eudigitalservicesact
#tiktok

items.title: How secure are Kurdish rights and identity under Syria's current Islamist government? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Residents of northeastern Syria are adjusting to a new reality following a US-brokered ceasefire between the Syrian government and the Kurdish-led Syrian Democratic Forces, the SDF. The deal was reached after weeks of fighting in the region led to major territory losses for Kurdish troops. Under the agreement, Kurdish forces will gradually be integrated into the Syrian army -- and Kurdish civil bodies will become part of the state. Since then, a small number of Syrian government forces have entered Kurdish strongholds – with the stated goal of facilitating this integration. But the situation is volatile - and many Kurds are skeptical about what they perceive as the erosion of their de facto autonomy. 

Chapter Breakdown
0:00 Syrian gov't forces move into Kurdish strongholds
0:46 DW’s Mohamad Chreyteh and Baderkhan Ahmad report on the mood among the Arab and Kurdish communities in northeast Syria  
4:22 Wladimir van Wilgenburg, journalist and author of "The Kurds of Northern Syria," on the shaky truce between Syrian government and Kurdish forces
